text: The patient is positioned semi-prone for the thoracoscopic esophagectomy. Four ports are visible: two 10 mm ports in the posterior axillary line at the fifth and ninth intercostal spaces, and two 5 mm ports in the mid-axillary line at the third and seventh intercostal spaces. The camera port is labeled 'C', the working ports are labeled 'W1' and 'W2', and the assistant port is labeled 'A'.

The surgeon begins the dissection at the inferior pulmonary ligament, retracting the lung anteriorly. Using surgical instruments, they cut through the ligament, aiming to remove fibro fatty tissue and lymph nodes along with the specimen. The inferior pulmonary vein is identified as the first anatomical structure in this step.

The assistant provides traction by pulling the lung anteriorly, while the surgeon applies counter traction by retracting the specimen posteriorly. Tiny lymph nodes are visible as part of the specimen, and a clear avascular plane is present between the inferior pulmonary vein and the specimen, containing fibrofatty tissue.

The surgeon performs entry dissection, carefully separating all tissue with the specimen from the inferior pulmonary vein and pericardium. A clear avascular plane is visible along the pericardium, extending from the hiatus to the subcarinal lymph node basin, guiding the dissection towards the opposite pleura. Sharp dissection techniques are employed.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to perform precise dissection, maintaining a dry surgical field. During the esophagectomy for cancer, all lymph nodes are removed en bloc with the specimen.

The surgeon performs mesoesophageal accession, taking all fibrophytic tissue with the specimen while maintaining a clear plane along the pericardium up to the hiatus. The nodal basin above the hiatus is addressed, ensuring no lymph nodes are left behind despite the prominence of the inferior vena cava. The contralateral pleura is exposed, marking the limit of the dissection.

The surgeon carefully safeguards the inferior pulmonary vein while dissecting two lymph node basins situated above and below it on the left side. The pleura over the right bronchus is incised, exposing the area for further dissection. The surgical field is kept dry to enhance visualization of small and critical structures.

The surgeon dissects lymph nodes from station 108 away from the right bronchus, incorporating them into the specimen. The assistant retracts the right bronchus downward anteriorly, while the surgeon provides counter traction by lifting tissue posteriorly, revealing a clear dissection plane. The vagus nerve to the bronchus is identified and preserved during the procedure.

The surgeon selectively cuts the vagus nerve branches entering the esophagus while preserving its bronchial branches. The right bronchial artery is clearly visualized, and its branches leading to the esophagus are being selectively cut.

The surgeon separates the esophagus from the trachea, noting the absence of fat between them, and dissects flush to the esophagus. The left border of the trachea is visible, serving as a landmark for the dissection. The left bronchus is defined and delineated, and once the dissection along the tracheobronchial tree is completed, the focus shifts to posterior dissection.

During the posterior dissection, the surgeon stays flush to the azygous vein and makes a posterior incision on the mediastinal pleura. Fibrophytic tissue is carefully removed from the azygous vein, and the prevascular fascia is dissected to reveal the descending aorta. The fat containing the thoracic duct is lifted away from the descending aorta and azygous vein, allowing for the thoracic duct to be clipped and cut. The dissection continues with all tissue being separated from the descending aorta.

The surgeon dissects up to the contralateral pleura, removing the station 112 lymph node with the specimen. A clear plane of separation is visible between the mesoesophagus and the left pleura. The pleura is kept intact unless accidentally opened, in which case the opening is enlarged at the end of the thoracoscopic phase to avoid placing a contralateral intercostal drainage tube. Following the completion of infra-axiacus dissection, the procedure transitions to supra-axiacus dissection.

The surgeon performs recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection by first defining the superior vena cava and making a pleural incision along it. The dissection reaches the apex of the chest, revealing the right subclavian artery. The right recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible, arising from the vagus nerve and encircling the subclavian artery. The surgeon then separates the specimen from the trachea, progressing towards the neck.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel, carefully tilting the active blade away from the membranous portion of the trachea to avoid injury. Dissection proceeds along the right side of the vagus nerve, which is visible crossing the trachea and extending towards the right subclavian artery. Station 106 tracheobronchial lymph node is located between the fork formed by the trachea and the vagus nerve.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to remove fibro-fatty tissue surrounding the right recurrent laryngeal lymph node group, ensuring the area remains dry for clear visibility. The right recurrent laryngeal lymph node is visible as part of the specimen, and pauses are taken to allow the active blade to cool down, preventing thermal injury.

The surgeon uses scissors to define and delineate the fibers of the right recurrent laryngeal nerve, cutting fibers that extend into the specimen to reveal the main trunk of the nerve. Posteriorly situated lymph nodes are included in the right lymph node packet. The combination of an energy source and scissors aids in the dissection, requiring patience and vigilance for any anomalous branches. The dissection extends to the upper limit at the inferior thyroid artery, where esophageal branches are visible, ensuring the entire lymph node packet is removed as one specimen.

The surgeon completes the right recurrent dissection, removing the specimen en masse with the station 106 recurrent lymph node. The procedure then shifts to the left side recurrent dissection, where the specimen is separated from the left side of the trachea using the tracheal dip maneuver. Fibro-fatty tissue is carefully dissected away from the trachea, revealing the visceral fascia covering the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node. The left recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible, and the AP window lymph node is gradually separated from the trachea and the main pulmonary artery. The AP window lymph nodes are arranged in a single file pattern, requiring diligent removal to ensure complete extraction.

The surgeon performs suprazygous lymph node dissection, with the descending aorta visible posteriorly and the trachea anteriorly. The AP window is cleared, and lymph nodes are attached to the specimen. Both the left and right recurrent laryngeal nerves are visible. This is the final view at the end of the surgery closure.

The surgeon performs an Intrathoracic Minimal Invasive Ivor Lewis esophagectomy with intrathoracic anastomosis. The esophagus is prepared for anastomosis by separating lymph nodes and pulling the specimen from the abdomen. The thoracic phase begins with port placement, and the esophagus is transected at the appropriate site. A utility incision is made in the seventh intercostal space to extract the specimen. The gastric conduit is prepared using staplers, ensuring a good gastric margin. The upper end of the esophagus is prepared for anastomosis, and a side-to-side stapled anastomosis is performed. The surgeon applies the first endoGI stapler for the anastomosis, ensuring at least six to seven centimeters of mobile esophagus for a tension-free connection. Additional staplers complete the entrotomy. A negative suction drain is placed alongside the ICD to drain the conduit, concluding the surgery.
